timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Letter to the Exiles

sections.header: Verses 1-14 -- "Seek the Welfare of the City"

heb.paragraph: "Seek the shalom of the city where I have carried you into exile. Pray to the LORD for it, because if it prospers, you too will prosper" (v.7). This command is revolutionary: seek the welfare of Babylon -- the very city that destroyed your homeland. Shalom here means comprehensive flourishing, not merely absence of conflict. The exiles are to be agents of blessing in the empire that enslaved them.

"For I know the plans I have for you," declares the LORD, "plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope (tiqwa) and a future" (v.11). The word tiqwa derives from the root qwh ("to wait, to hope"). This is not optimism but eschatological expectation -- a future grounded in God's sovereign purpose rather than present circumstances.

refs.note: "Live such good lives among the pagans that they may see your good deeds and glorify God" -- the NT equivalent of seeking the shalom of the city.
Daniel faithfully serves the Babylonian and Persian empires while maintaining his prayer life -- the lived realization of Jeremiah's instructions.
"Our citizenship is in heaven" -- the exile paradigm shapes NT ecclesiology: the church lives faithfully in a world that is not its permanent home.

notes.note: The instructions: build houses, plant gardens, marry, have children, multiply. Normal life in abnormal circumstances. Most radically: "Seek the shalom of the city where I have carried you." The enemy city is to be blessed.
Warning against false prophets in exile who promise quick return. "They are prophesying lies to you in my name. I have not sent them."
The seventy-year timeline (from ch. 25) is reiterated. The plans God has are "plans to prosper and not to harm." But the prosperity is seventy years away, not imminent.

"You will seek me and find me when you seek me with all your heart." The condition for divine encounter is wholehearted seeking. This echoes Deuteronomy 4:29 and anticipates Jesus's "seek and you will find" (Matt 7:7).

Calvin develops a theology of civic responsibility: "Believers are not to withdraw from the world but to serve it. Even in exile -- even under hostile government -- the people of God seek the common good."

Calvin warns against misreading this verse as individual prosperity: "The promise is to the community, across seventy years of suffering. It is a word of hope in the darkest hour, not a guarantee of personal comfort."
The command to "pray for" Babylon is remarkable -- intercessory prayer for the oppressor nation. This anticipates Jesus's command to pray for enemies (Matt 5:44).
"When seventy years are completed for Babylon" -- the Hebrew can be read as "for" or "at" Babylon, i.e., the seventy years may refer to Babylon's dominance rather than the length of the exile itself.

Lundbom identifies the letter as "exile theology in practical form." The instructions translate the good-figs theology of chapter 24 into daily life: the exiles are not under curse but under commission. Babylon is their assignment, not their punishment.

Brueggemann calls "seek the shalom of the city" the "most subversive sentence in the Hebrew Bible." It requires the conquered to bless the conqueror, the displaced to invest in the displacing city. This is not passivity but active, countercultural faithfulness.

hist.context: The letter to the Babylonian exiles is one of the most pastorally significant documents in the Hebrew Bible. Written probably in 594 BC, it addresses a community in crisis: torn from their homeland, surrounded by a foreign culture, tempted by false prophets who promise imminent return. Jeremiah's counsel is shockingly counterintuitive: settle down, build houses, plant gardens, marry, and seek the welfare of Babylon. The exile is not a parenthesis to endure but a context for faithful living. The famous promise of 29:11 must be read in this context -- it is not a prosperity promise but a hope grounded in the seventy-year framework.

sections.header: Verses 15-32 -- Against the False Prophets in Exile

heb.paragraph: The false prophets in Babylon -- Ahab son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah son of Maaseiah -- are condemned for prophesying sheqer (lies) and committing adultery (v.23). The signature sin of Jeremiah appears even in exile: institutional liars who give God's name to their own fantasies. Shemaiah the Nehelamite (v.24) writes back to Jerusalem demanding Jeremiah's arrest.

refs.note: The parallel charges against Jerusalem's false prophets: adultery and lies. The same sins corrupt the prophetic office in both locations.
"The time will come when people will not endure sound teaching" -- the NT continuation of the perennial conflict between popular comfort and divine truth.

notes.note: Ahab and Zedekiah will be "roasted in the fire" by Nebuchadnezzar -- a gruesome execution. Their names will become a byword for cursing among the exiles.
Shemaiah's letter demands Jeremiah's punishment. The temple establishment in Jerusalem is urged to silence the prophet from across the empire -- persecution by mail.
God's verdict on Shemaiah: "He has preached rebellion (sara) against the LORD." The same charge laid against Hananiah (28:16). Both false prophets are guilty of sedition against divine governance.

Calvin observes that false prophecy is classified as rebellion -- not mere error but willful resistance to God's declared purpose. The distinction between honest mistake and deliberate deception is crucial.
Being "roasted in the fire" may refer to execution by burning -- attested in Babylonian legal practice for certain offenses, including rebellion.
Lundbom notes that the false prophets in exile mirror the false prophets in Jerusalem. The geographic change has not produced spiritual change. Lies travel as easily as truth.

Brueggemann reads Shemaiah's letter as evidence that "the institutional impulse to silence dissent knows no borders." Even from Babylon, the establishment reaches back to Jerusalem to suppress the prophetic voice.

hist.context: The second half of the letter addresses false prophets operating among the exiles. Ahab and Zedekiah will be handed over to Nebuchadnezzar and executed (v.21-22). Shemaiah writes to Jerusalem's temple authorities demanding they silence Jeremiah. God's response: Shemaiah and his descendants will be excluded from the restoration community. The conflict between true and false prophecy follows Israel even into exile.

rows.label: vv.10-14
rows.text: "I Know the Plans I Have for You"

lit.note: The letter to the exiles is the most pastorally significant document in the book. "Seek the shalom of the city" transforms exile from punishment into commission.

themes.note: Unusually balanced -- Mercy and Faith both at 7 alongside Judgment. Mission at 8: "seek the shalom of the city" redefines the exile as a mission field.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Letter to the Exiles

translations.text: He entrusted the letter to Elasah son of Shaphan and to Gemariah son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to King Nebuchadnezzar in Babylon. It said:
The letter was sent by the hand of Elasah the son of Shaphan and Gemariah the son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to Babylon to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. It said:
(This was after King Jehoiachin and the queen mother, the court officials and the leaders of Judah and Jerusalem, the skilled workers and the artisans had gone into exile from Jerusalem.)
This was after King Jeconiah and the queen mother, the eunuchs, the officials of Judah and Jerusalem, the craftsmen, and the metal workers had departed from Jerusalem.

thread.text: "Live such good lives among the pagans that they may see your good deeds and glorify God" -- the NT equivalent of seeking the shalom of the city.
Daniel faithfully serves the Babylonian and Persian empires while maintaining his prayer life -- the lived realization of Jeremiah's instructions.
"Our citizenship is in heaven" -- the exile paradigm shapes NT ecclesiology: the church lives faithfully in a world that is not its permanent home.
The parallel charges against Jerusalem's false prophets: adultery and lies. The same sins corrupt the prophetic office in both locations.
"The time will come when people will not endure sound teaching" -- the NT continuation of the perennial conflict between popular comfort and divine truth.

coaching.tip: 'Seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to the LORD on its behalf' (v. 7). Exiles are called to invest in enemy territory — build houses, plant gardens, pray for Babylon. This is neither assimilation nor resistance but a third way: faithful presence. The verse shaped Jewish diaspora theology and anticipates the church's posture in a non-Christian world.

'I know the plans I have for you — plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future' (v. 11). This beloved verse is addressed to exiles who will wait seventy years. The 'plans' include decades of displacement. Prosperity isn't immediate comfort — it's long-term faithfulness. Reading this verse without its context turns a marathon promise into a sprint guarantee.
